title: Feminism
wikitext:
[]: Feminism             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Thu Jul 31, 2008 7:19 am I'm not suggesting loading the show with another theme, but how would characterizing someone as a feminist work? It's very female while at the same time anti-feminine, or at least against the traditional definition. A little like how Asuka came across as a female chauvinist. Maybe Shinko could be an angry feminist.

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 8:59 am Are you suggesting that NGE is feminist? If so, I'd say you're wrong. There are as many male characters as female characters, and the female characters are shown to have equal capabilities to the male ones (they're not portrayed as better in any way). Sure, the Evas are female, but they had to be some gender, right? However, they're really no different than if they had been male. The only clue we have to the Evas' gender (as far as actions go) is their protectiveness, such as when Unit 1 blocked the falling lights in the first episode. But even then, that's a stereotype of women/mothers (albeit an accurate one), and IIRC, feminists aren't too thrilled about stereotypes. I'll admit NGE does portray almost every female character (Maya and Rei being the only exceptions that I can think of) as very strong, very assertive, very commanding can-do women. But that's to be expected; they're in a military organization, and you'd better be tough for that. The only female we see that's not affiliated with NERV is the class rep, meaning that it's only natural for her to have similar qualities. So no, NGE isn't really feminist. Are the women typical women? No, but nothing's really "typical" in an anime. Do the women behave logically considering their environment? I'd say so.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu Jul 31, 2008 9:16 am Gamer_2k4 wrote: Are you suggesting that NGE is feminist? If so, I'd say you're wrong. There are as many male characters as female characters, and the female characters are shown to have equal capabilities to the male ones (they're not portrayed as better in any way).

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 9:57 am Xard wrote: You do know feminism = men and women are equal instead of feminism = WOMYN ARE SUPERIOR, right?

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 9:58 am Xard wrote: You do know feminism = men and women are equal instead of feminism = WOMYN ARE SUPERIOR, right?

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 10:06 am I didn't say this before, Xard, but the issue is that the angry, overzealous, overreactionary extreme feminists do have a superiority complex, and they are more vocal, more obnoxious, and easier to spot in a crowd, so they get more media coverage. Therefore, they have become the public perception of Feminism. Which, of course, only hurts the movement.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 10:43 am Formless One wrote: I didn't say this before, Xard, but the issue is that the angry, overzealous, overreactionary extreme feminists do have a superiority complex, and they are more vocal, more obnoxious, and easier to spot in a crowd, so they get more media coverage. <snip>

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 10:52 am THE Hal E. Burton 9000 wrote: though if Eva is feminist, it still has the self-hatred problem, so would that make Eva misogynist too??

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 10:55 am I'd chalk it up to the series gender bending. All the characters are taken from common character types in other robot shows, but with the gender flipped. And since all the other robot shows are filled with macho guys piloting huge penis-compensators, you can be forgiven for thinking the show is feminist, because all those other robot shows border on the opposite end of that spectrum. Add the fact that Eva deconstructs the genre and its characters, making them extremely flawed human beings, and it seems as if its on both ends of the spectrum at once ! In the end, though, remember that Asuka was always bent out of shape because of Shinji, a guy, was a better pilot than her. Take that as you will.

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 11:03 am Formless One wrote: In the end, though, remember that Asuka was always bent out of shape because of Shinji, a guy, was a better pilot than her. Take that as you will.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 11:07 am Rei, a better pilot than Asuka? I think not! She was upset because Rei saved her life when Asuka was at her weakest moment and couldn't do anything to save herself.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 31, 2008 11:24 am Character-wise, NGE is feminist. But the fact is that the ideal of feminism (men and women are equal) has kinda become the norm over the years so I'm not sure if we can even look at NGE in a feminist context. However, the female archetypal portrayal in NGE is anything but feminist. It plays on the anima archetype and portrays woman as the Goddess. There tends to be two types of common portrayals of women by males in film: one is of the enigmatic Goddess that can't be controlled but is simply a force of nature, and the other is of the diminutive type that's helpless and needs rescuing. Things over the years have leveled out some, but female portrayals by male film-makers and writers have always been sketchy.

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 12:59 pm Formless One wrote: Rei, a better pilot than Asuka? I think not! She was upset because Rei saved her life when Asuka was at her weakest moment and couldn't do anything to save herself.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jul 31, 2008 2:55 pm This thread needs more ZapX. Anyway: Xard wrote: You do know feminism = men and women are equal instead of feminism = WOMYN ARE SUPERIOR, right?

[]:             Quote Post by swamp147 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:06 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Even Rei, who's stereotypically the ideal Japanese woman is "strong". Though she wishes for death (and Lilith physically dies in the end), Rei does the right thing by guiding Shinji throughout Instrumentality.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:13 pm QFT. Not all of us are into that 60s extremism bra burning, man hating, bullshit.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:29 pm Rei is an "ideal Japanese woman"? If so, then which one?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:30 pm Formless One wrote: That said, I would probably agree that Eva was feminist. Japan is notorious for having some rather obsolete cultural attitudes towards women, and I imagine that that is part of the motivation for the series gender bending: as a way to highlight the cultural bias/attitude.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Thu Jul 31, 2008 3:42 pm True, but there are always exceptions in any culture, people that won't put up with crap, especially when it has affected them personally. A cursory scanning of that wiki article shows that this was the case; Kenji Mizoguchi's father sold Kenji's sister as a geisha, i.e. a prostitute/sex slave (don't know exactly which side of the line that one falls) So naturally, that makes it a personal perogative of Kenji's, not necessarily of the culture that spawned him.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Thu Jul 31, 2008 4:12 pm @ Gamer_2k4, I was joking there and what SSD-chan said, especially about MOAR Zappy
